coredata.dc:title: Phosphorescence approach based on silica protected carbon dots for autofluorescence interference-free and highly selective detection of fluoride
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Meng-Xian
Chen, Xiao-Bing
Liu, Wen-Ye
Wang, Jian-Hua
Yu, Yong-Liang
Chen, Shuai
coredata.dc:description:
Background Fluoride (F−), an anion with the smallest ionic radius and highest charge density, plays an important role in biomedical and environmental processes, making the development of accurate F− detection methods of great importance. Fluorometric methods with simplicity and sensitivity have gained considerable attention in F− detection. However, their accuracy faces challenges due to issues like autofluorescence interference during real-time light excitation and limited selectivity. Therefore, it is important to establish a simple, real-time light excitation-free, and highly selective method for the accurate determination of F− in complicated samples.
Results Herein, a novel phosphorescent approach is developed for the selective and accurate detection of F− in complex samples. Phosphorescence emission CDs@SiO2 is fabricated by confining CDs in a silica protective layer. This design retains the favorable water solubility of silica while benefitting from its inertness, making it resistant to most substances. Furthermore, phosphorescent analysis without real-time light excitation eliminates autofluorescence interference, significantly improving the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and simplifying sample pretreatment. The specific interaction between F− and the Si–O bond can lead to the degradation of the silica protective layer, exposing the CDs to the solution, resulting in phosphorescence quenching, achieving the highly accurate and sensitive detection of F− with a linear range of 0.001–4 mM and a limit of detection (LOD) of 1 μM.
Significance This novel F− phosphorescence method based on the metal-free phosphorescent nanomaterial CDs@SiO2 integrates the benefits of no autofluorescence interference, high selectivity, and full aqueous compatibility, and its combination with a smartphone provides a simple, portable, and cost-effective detection platform for accurate and highly sensitive determination of F− in complex samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phosphorescence
Fluoride
Autofluorescence interference-free
High selectivity
Visual readout

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Phosphorescence detection of F− in real samples
Feasibility and principle of CDs@SiO2 based phosphorescence approach for F−
Analytical performance of CDs@SiO2 based phosphorescence approach for F−
Smartphone-based smart approach for F− detection
Detection of F− in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21922402 and 21874017 ), Liaoning Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China ( 2021-MS-099 ), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( N2205004 ). Special thanks are due to the instrumental analysis from Analytical and Testing Center, Northeastern University (China).

This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21922402 and 21874017), Liaoning Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2021-MS-099), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N2205004). Special thanks are due to the instrumental analysis from Analytical and Testing Center, Northeastern University (China).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and particle size distribution histogram of CDs. (B) UV/vis absorption, phosphorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs and CDs@SiO2. (C) FTIR spectra of CDs and CDs@SiO2. (D) TEM image and particle size distribution histogram of CDs@SiO2. (E) The photographs of CDs and CDs@SiO2 aqueous solutions with 365 nm UV light on and off. (F) Deconvoluted high-resolution Si 2p XPS spectrum of CDs@SiO2.

(A) Phosphorescence intensities of CDs, CDs@SiO2, and CDs@SiO2 reacted with 10 mM F− (inset: photos of (a) 20 mg mL−1 CDs, (b) 1 mg mL−1 CDs@SiO2, and (c) 1 mg mL−1 CDs@SiO2 reacted with 10 mM F− with 365 nm UV light off). (B) Phosphorescence lifetime decay scatterplot of CDs@SiO2 before and after reaction with 10 mM F−. TEM images and size distribution histograms of CDs@SiO2 after reaction with (C) 5 mM and (D) 10 mM F−, respectively.

Effects of (A) TEOS volume, (B) pH, (C) reaction temperature, and (D) reaction time on the relative phosphorescence intensity of CDs@SiO2 upon reaction with F− (2 mM) at 510 nm (P0 and P refer to the phosphorescence intensity of the solution before and after the reaction with F− under the same experimental conditions).

(A) Phosphorescence spectra of the CDs@SiO2 (100 μg mL−1) after reaction with different concentrations of F−. (B) The linear relationship between relative phosphorescence intensities and the F− concentration. (C) Relative phosphorescence intensities of CDs@SiO2 after reaction with 2 mM F− or 2 mM F− and different interferences (1: Blank, 2: F−, 3: F− + Cl−, 4: F− + Br−, 5: F− + I−, 6: F− + SCN−, 7: F− + NO3 −, 8: F− + SO4 2−, 9: F− + HCO3 −, 10: F− + HPO4 2−, 11: F− + CH3COO−, 12: F− + H2PO4 −, 13: F− + S2−, 14: F− + S2O3 2−, 15: F− + Mg2+, 16: F− + K+, 17: F− + Zn2+, 18: F− + Glucose, 19: F− + Cys, 20: F− + sucrose, 21: F− + UA, 22: F− + DA, 23: F− + AA, 24: F− + Glu, 25: F− + Ala, 26: F− + Asp, 27: F− + Leu, 28: F− + GSH, 29: F− + thiourea). (D) Relative phosphorescence and fluorescence background signals of various dilutions of HAc-NaAc buffer, Colgate, LESENING mouthwash, serum, and urine, respectively (I/I0 refers to the ratio of the background signal of the diluted actual sample to that of the buffer).

(A) Diagram of F− smart detection device. (B) Phosphorescence image and (C) the corresponding G/R values of test solutions for varying concentrations of F−. (D) The linear relationship between the G/R values and the F− concentrations.
(A) Synthesis of phosphorescent CDs@SiO2. (B) Mechanism and (C) schematic illustration of F− detection using the smartphone-based detection device.
Determination results of F− in real samples (n = 3).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The contents of F− marked on the product package.

head.ce:title: Phosphorescence approach based on silica protected carbon dots for autofluorescence interference-free and highly selective detection of fluoride

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang, 110819, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang, 110819, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluoride (F−), an anion with the smallest ionic radius and highest charge density, plays an important role in biomedical and environmental processes, making the development of accurate F− detection methods of great importance. Fluorometric methods with simplicity and sensitivity have gained considerable attention in F− detection. However, their accuracy faces challenges due to issues like autofluorescence interference during real-time light excitation and limited selectivity. Therefore, it is important to establish a simple, real-time light excitation-free, and highly selective method for the accurate determination of F− in complicated samples.

Herein, a novel phosphorescent approach is developed for the selective and accurate detection of F− in complex samples. Phosphorescence emission CDs@SiO2 is fabricated by confining CDs in a silica protective layer. This design retains the favorable water solubility of silica while benefitting from its inertness, making it resistant to most substances. Furthermore, phosphorescent analysis without real-time light excitation eliminates autofluorescence interference, significantly improving the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and simplifying sample pretreatment. The specific interaction between F− and the Si–O bond can lead to the degradation of the silica protective layer, exposing the CDs to the solution, resulting in phosphorescence quenching, achieving the highly accurate and sensitive detection of F− with a linear range of 0.001–4 mM and a limit of detection (LOD) of 1 μM.

This novel F− phosphorescence method based on the metal-free phosphorescent nanomaterial CDs@SiO2 integrates the benefits of no autofluorescence interference, high selectivity, and full aqueous compatibility, and its combination with a smartphone provides a simple, portable, and cost-effective detection platform for accurate and highly sensitive determination of F− in complex samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluoride (F−), an anion featuring the smallest ionic radius and highest charge density, is closely related to biomedical and environmental processes [1]. However, the dissolution of natural minerals or the discharge of F−-containing waste often leads to relatively high F− concentrations in groundwater. Excessive intake of F− can potentially cause kidney infections, immune system damage, and inhibit or cause death in the growth of plants and animals [2,3]. Therefore, the development of simple and accurate F− detection methods is of great importance to protect ecosystems and public health.

Several conventional methods have been proposed for the determination of F−, for example, the ion-selective electrode method is characterized by excellent selectivity and broad applicability with straightforward operation, although careful consideration of environmental factors is essential [4]. In contrast, ion chromatography offers simplicity and speed but is restricted by equipment costs and susceptibility to complex sample interferences [5]. Colorimetric methods, recognized for their rapidity and simplicity, might exhibit limited sensitivity and could be susceptible to interference from colored samples [6]. Fluorescence method with simplicity and high sensitivity has raised tremendous attention in F− detection [7–9]. However, it usually requires real-time light excitation, and complex sample matrices such as urine and serum often contain biomolecules such as sugars and proteins that can produce autofluorescence, thus affecting the detection accuracy [10]. In addition, the large hydration enthalpy of F−, the same charge and similar ionic radius as OH− (1.285 Å for F− vs. 1.32 Å for OH−) make its selective sensing in aqueous solutions a great challenge [11]. Although some organic probes have exhibited favorable F− selectivity, they are often limited by poor water solubility, allowing only the detection of F− in organic-water mixed solutions [7]. Therefore, it is important and challenging to establish a simple, real-time light excitation-free, and highly selective method for the accurate determination of F− in complicated aqueous samples.

Phosphorescence probes can slowly release trapped carriers, resulting in long-lasting phosphorescence without real-time light excitation [12]. Phosphorescence analysis is therefore of great interest in sensing as it effectively avoids autofluorescence interference, simplifies sample pretreatment, and achieves high signal-to-noise ratios (SNR) [10,13–17]. Although some iridium(III) complex-based F− phosphorescent methods have been constructed, these probes are limited to the detection of organic fluoride compounds within organic–aqueous mixed solution [18–20]. However, water F− contamination typically refers to free inorganic F−, such as NaF. Carbon dots (CDs) with favorable photophysical properties and environmental friendliness have been utilized to construct fully water-soluble phosphorescence probes by encapsulating them in silica shells [21]. To our delight, silica is well hydrophilic and strongly inert, while F− is one of the rare chemicals capable of etching and degrading silica [22], making it a promising candidate for the selective quenching of probe phosphorescence. Therefore, we speculate that it is a potentially attractive F− sensing strategy that realizes a highly specific response to F− and integrates the advantages of phosphorescent analysis without autofluorescence interference, promising accurate detection of F− in complex aqueous samples.

Here, we developed a phosphorescence approach based on silica protected CDs (CDs@SiO2) for highly selective and accurate detection of F− in complex aqueous samples (Scheme 1 ). The water-soluble phosphorescence CDs@SiO2 was synthesized by confining CDs within silica substrates. The hydrophilic silica layer effectively restricts the rotation and vibration of CD bonds and protects it against water molecules and dissolved oxygen to enable water-soluble phosphorescence, and benefits from its inertness to resist most substances. Upon exposure to F−, the specific etching action of F− on the silica layer triggers the degradation of the silica layer and the re-exposure of CDs to the aqueous solution, leading to phosphorescence quenching and thus achieving F− detection. The integration of a smartphone and a camera obscura allows for the development of a portable detection device for the convenient detection of F− in real samples. The innovative CDs@SiO2 phosphorescence approach offers enhanced selectivity, autofluorescence interference-free, and compatibility with portable devices, enabling precise determination of F− in complex aqueous samples.

CDs were prepared by microwave method referring to the previous method [21]. 1.2 mL of ethylenediamine (EDA) and 2 mL of H3PO4 were added to 14.8 mL of deionized water. After mixing thoroughly, they were transferred to a microwave, and heated at 800 W for 140 s. After cooling to room temperature, 20 mL of deionized water was added, and they were centrifugated at 6000 rpm for 10 min to remove large impurities. Then filtrated through a 0.22 μm filter membrane and dialyzed (MWCO: 500 Da) for 7 days to obtain pale yellow CDs powder after freeze-drying.

0.015 mL of tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and 4.5 mL of deionized water were added to 0.5 mL of 25 mg mL−1 CDs aqueous solution, then added 0.015 mL of NH3·H2O dropwise while stirring. Then, they were stirred and reacted at 25 °C for 8 h. Next, they were centrifugated at 12,000 rpm for 15 min to remove large particles and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to obtain a transparent CDs@SiO2 solution, and stored at 4 °C for further use.

50 μL of F− standard solution with different concentrations was added to 10 μL of CDs@SiO2 (2 mg mL−1) and 140 μL of HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH = 2). The phosphorescence intensity at 510 nm of the test solution was counted after reaction at 55 °C for 4 h.

Anions, metal ions, and biomolecules commonly found in actual tap water, urine, and serum samples including Cl− (10.5 mM), Br− (9 mM), I− (0.63 μM), SCN− (6.46 μM), NO3 − (76.6 μM), SO4 2− (1.5 mg mL−1), HCO3 − (1.5 μM), HPO4 2− (0.5 mM), CH3COO− (1 mM), H2PO4 − (0.5 mM), S2− (10 mM), S2O3 2− (10 mM), Mg2+ (1.152 mM), K+ (5.5 mM), Zn2+ (28 μM), glucose (6 mM), Cys (1.1 mg mL−1), sucrose (40 μM), uric acid (UA, 0.4 mM), dopamine (DA, 1.7 μM), ascorbic acid (AA, 120 μM), Glutamic acid (Glu, 1 mM), Alanine (Ala, 1 mM), Aspartic acid (Asp, 1 mM), Leucine (Leu, 1 mM), Glutathione (GSH, 10 mM), and thiourea (10 mM) were selected as interferences to evaluate the selectivity of the approach. It should be pointed out that, in order to simulate actual detection conditions, the concentrations of interferences are match or exceed than those in real tap water, urine, and serum samples. The solution preparation was the same as that of F− detection except that 50 μL of each interfering substance was added to the solution.

The smart detection device consists of a 365 nm UV lamp (18 W), a portable camera obscura, and a smartphone. Black resin assembled camera obscura was chosen to provide a stable measurement environment with minimal interference from the unstable natural environment. The excitation light comes from an economical 365 nm UV lamp. The green (G) and red (R) values of the phosphorescence images were extracted using the built-in Swatches App to obtain a standard curve of the ratio of G to R values (G/R) versus F− concentration. The concentration of F− in the sample is obtained by substituting the G/R value of the test solutions into the standard linear equation.

Given that the phosphorescence intensity of the test solution gradually decreases with time when the excitation light is switched off, resulting in a phosphorescence image that is closely related to the photographing time. Therefore, to ensure the reliability and reproducibility of the method, a video of the test solution was recorded with the 365 nm UV light on and off, and divided the video into frames at a rate of 30 frames per second (fps) by a video editor-CapCut to accurately identify the image at the moment the excitation light was switched off (image N. 9 in Fig. S1A), and the 4th frame image following (image N. 13 in Fig. S1A) was selected as the phosphorescence image of the test solution to extract the G and R values for the calculation of the F− concentration. In addition, the average intensity of images N. 13−30 did gradually decrease over time (Fig. S1B), indicating the reliability of this phosphorescent image-identifying method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phosphorescence detection of F− in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: 20 μL of mouthwash from Colgate or LESENING was added to 50 μL of CDs@SiO2 (2 mg mL−1) and 130 μL of HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 2). Phosphorescence images were collected after 4 h of reaction at 55 °C. The content of F− was calculated by substituting the G/R value of the real samples into the standard linear equation. Urine and serum were collected from healthy volunteers (Northeastern University School Hospital, Shenyang, China). Tap water was collected from Northeastern University (China). 20 μL of urine, 20 μL of tap water, or 10 μL of serum was added to 50 μL of CDs@SiO2 (2 mg mL−1) and different concentrations of F− standard solution. Then, they were diluted with HAc-NaAc buffer solutions (pH = 2) to 200 μL. Data acquisition and calculation methods were the same as for the mouthwash samples.

Heteroatom doping can promote intersystem crossing (ISC) and lead to effectively populated triplet excitons, so EDA and H3PO4 were chosen as carbon sources to prepare CDs. Water is a polar molecule and can interact with excited states through various mechanisms, including hydrogen bonding and electron or energy transfer processes, leading to non-radiative deactivation of the excited state. Additionally, when a molecule in its first excited triplet state encounters an oxygen molecule (3O2), energy can be transferred from the excited molecule to the oxygen molecule, resulting in the formation of singlet oxygen (1O2) and returning the excited molecule to its ground state without emission. Recognized as triplet-triplet annihilation, is a common non-radiative deactivation pathway in the presence of dissolved oxygen. Moreover, the vibration and rotation of the chemical bonds of the CDs also lead to non-radiative deactivation and thus to a weakening of the intensity of the phosphorescence. Therefore, when the CDs are confined and isolated into the silica shell, apart from isolating the CDs from oxygen in the aqueous solution, the silica layer also restricts the vibration and rotation of the CD bonds, thereby reducing the nonradiative decay rate of triplet excitons and ultimately enabling phosphorescent emission in the aqueous solution with a quantum yield of 8.49 % (Fig. S2 and Table S1) [21,23,24]. The morphology of the CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM), and the result shows that the CDs are quasi-spherical and display distinct lattice stripes with an average diameter of about 2.13 nm (Fig. 1 A). Consistently, their X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern exhibits several sharp peaks at 2θ of approximately 16, 20, 22, and 23°, further confirming their highly ordered structure (Fig. S3) [25,26]. In addition, the CDs exhibit only solid-state phosphorescence emission but without phosphorescence in aqueous solution due to the attack of water molecules and dissolved oxygen as well as energy dissipation caused by vibration and rotation of bonds in CDs (Fig. 1B and E, and S4). [21] [ [27,28]], The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of the CDs shows a broad peak around 3415 cm−1, indicating an abundance of hydroxyl groups on the CD surface (Fig. 1C) [29]. Thus, CDs can be subjected to a dehydration condensation reaction with TEOS via the classical Stöber strategy to form a hydrophilic silica protective layer on their surface, leading to phosphorescent emitting materials (CDs@SiO2) with satisfactory water solubility as well as long lifetimes (Fig. S5) [6,21]. TEM image of CDs@SiO2 show that they are still quasi-spherical, but increased in diameter to approximately 5.24 nm (Fig. 1D). Their XRD pattern does not show a sharp peak similar to that in CDs but rather a broad peak similar to that of silica nanoparticles at the 2θ of about 22.4°, further indicating that the CDs are successfully encapsulated into the amorphous silica layer (Fig. S6). The stretching vibrations at 3429, 1631, and 1087 cm−1 in CDs@SiO2 FTIR spectrum belong to Si–OH and Si–O–Si bonds [30,31]. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) full spectrum of CDs@SiO2 exhibits the characteristic binding energy band of Si element at 104 eV (Fig. S7B) [32]. Compared with CDs, the C 1s deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectrum of CDs@SiO2 shows distinct C–O–Si bond, the N 1s spectrum exhibits N–Si (402.3 eV) bond, and the O 1s spectrum possesses unique peaks belonging to Si-Ox (532.8 eV) and C–O–Si (533.4 eV) bonds (Fig. S7 C-H). In addition, the Si 2p spectrum of CDs@SiO2 can be deconvoluted into two peaks centred at 103.2 and 104.0 eV, attributed to the Si-Ox and Si–O–C bonds, respectively (Fig. 1F) [33,34]. The above results show that water-soluble phosphorescent CDs@SiO2 were successfully produced by encapsulating CDs in silica layer. In addition, the CDs@SiO2 displays satisfactory pH, temporal, temperature, and ionic strength stability (Figs. S8–11), indicating their satisfactory suitability for constructing F− phosphorescent methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility and principle of CDs@SiO2 based phosphorescence approach for F−
ce:section.ce:para:
In acidic environments, the strongly electronegative F− undergoes ionization equilibrium with H⁺, forming HF. Subsequently breaks the Si–O bond to form more stable Si–F bonds, effectively etching the silica shell (Reaction 1) [6,35–37].
(1)
S i
O 2
+ 6
H F
→ 2
H +
+
S i
F 6
2 −
+ 2
H 2
O

Therefore, it is reasonable to assume that the presence of F− would disrupt the Si–O bonds of silica layer outside CD@SiO2, leading to the re-exposure of CDs to the aqueous solution and resulting in a significant quenching of phosphorescence. As expected, after reacting with F− (10 mM), the phosphorescence of CDs@SiO2 at 510 nm was significantly reduced and the phosphorescence was barely observed in the corresponding images (inset c), confirming the responsiveness of the CDs@SiO2 towards F− (Fig. 2 A and Video S1).
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2023.342102

The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 2 Multimedia component 2

To gain a deeper understanding of the mechanism, the reaction products of CDs@SiO2 and F− were collected and subjected to lifetime characterization. The results in Fig. 2B show that the average phosphorescence lifetime of CDs@SiO2 is 1.39 s. However, after F− etching, it is significantly reduced to 2.04 ns, comparable to that of bare CDs (Fig. S12), suggesting that the protective silica layer of CDs@SiO2 may have been damaged, causing them to be re-exposed to the aqueous solution and resulting in phosphorescence quenching [21]. In addition, the XRD map of the reaction products shows sharp peaks similar to those of the bare CDs (Fig. S13), indicating that the silica shell of CDs@SiO2 had been significantly disrupted. To visually confirm whether the structure of CDs@SiO2 had indeed been altered, TEM characterization of the reaction products was performed. It is obvious from Fig. 2C that the size of CDs@SiO2 decreases from 5.24 nm to 3.70 nm after reaction with 5 mM F−. Moreover, after reaction with 10 mM F−, it further reduced to 2.33 nm, which is not significantly different from the size of the bare CDs (Figs. 2D and 1A), indicating that the silica layer of CDs@SiO2 is almost completely etched and the CDs is re-exposed to the aqueous solution. This may be attributed to the fact that under acidic conditions, F− with strong electronegativity is in ionization equilibrium with H+ to form HF, which further reacts with silica to etch Si–O bonds to form F–Si bonds [37], thereby damaging the protective silica layer of CDs@SiO2 and causing a decrease in phosphorescence. Based on the above results, it is expected that a phosphorescence approach based on CDs@SiO2 can be constructed to achieve high selectivity and accurate detection of F− in aqueous solutions.

The preparation of CDs@SiO2 with a suitable silica protective layer thickness is key to achieving highly sensitive F− detection. Therefore, optimization was performed by varying the key factor affecting the thickness of the shell layer, i.e., TEOS volume, and evaluating the corresponding phosphorescence quenching degree upon the addition of F− for optimal performance. As shown in Fig. 3 A, when the TEOS volume increased from 10 to 15 μL, the phosphorescence quenching degree caused by F− (2 mM) increased instead, probably because 10 μL of TEOS was insufficient for the complete encapsulation of the CDs to produce stable phosphorescence signals. While as the TEOS volume increased from 15 to 300 μL, the phosphorescence quenching degree gradually decreased, which might be due to the gradual thickening of the silica layer, making the effective etching caused by F− gradually decrease. However, when the volume of TEOS was further increased from 300 to 500 μL, there was no significant change in the phosphorescence signal, which may be due to the overly thick silica layer of CDs, which cannot be efficiently etched by F− to exposure the CDs. According to the above results, 15 μL of TEOS was chosen as the optimum volume for constructing CDs@SiO2 in the subsequent experiments to achieve optimal performance.

To achieve satisfactory detection performance, four other crucial aspects were examined, including pH, reaction temperature, time, and CDs@SiO2 concentration. As shown in Fig. 3B, consistent with expectations, adjusting the test solution pH to around 2 yielded the most effective etching effect of F− on the silica protection layer [6]. Additionally, increasing the reaction temperature from 25 to 75 °C led to a gradual and significant enhancement in phosphorescence quenching, with stabilization at 55 °C (Fig. 3C). Following the addition of 2 mM F− (pH = 2, 55 °C), the phosphorescence quenching reached its maximum after a 4-h reaction and remained stable with a further extension of the reaction time (Fig. 3D). It should be noted that the 4-h reaction time is not conducive to in situ analysis. Nevertheless, water-soluble phosphorescent probes are currently undergoing remarkable advancements. We are confident that the development of probes with higher quantum yields holds the potential to enable precise and rapid detection of F− using this method, thereby circumventing issues related to autofluorescence and complex sample pretreatment. In addition, the phosphorescence quenching degree increased gradually as the concentration of CDs@SiO2 decreased (Fig. S14A). When the CDs@SiO2 concentration is as low as 50 μg mL−1, although approximately 42 % phosphorescence quenching was achieved with 1 mM F−, the stability of the system was compromised (Fig. S14B). To strike a balance between stability and sensitivity, a probe concentration of 100 μg mL−1 was ultimately chosen. In addition, the CDs@SiO2 remains well stabilised during long incubations at 55 °C (Fig. S15). Consequently, subsequent experiments were conducted under the optimized conditions of 100 μg mL−1 CDs@SiO2, 55 °C, pH = 2, and a reaction time of 4 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of CDs@SiO2 based phosphorescence approach for F−
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal reaction conditions, the responses of the phosphorescent apporch to different concentrations of F− were investigated to evaluate its performance. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the phosphorescence intensity gradually decreased with increasing F− concentration, and there is an exact linear relationship between the relative phosphorescence intensity at 510 nm and the F− concentrations (Fig. 4B) in the range of 0.001–4 mM with a determined limit of detection (LOD) of 1 μM (S/N = 3), which is below the World Health Organization (WHO) maximum residue level of 79 μM [38]. In addition to high selectivity, this method also offers advantages in autofluorescence interference-free, high sensitivity, and 100 % water compatibility, demonstrating great potential for the determination of F− in complex aqueous samples (Table S2).

Anti-interference capability is crucial for accurate target detection, and it was thoroughly examined by introducing various anions, metal ions, and biomolecules commonly present in tap water, urine, and serum in the existence of F−. When only F− exists, the phosphorescence intensity of the solution is quenched to 40.0 % of the initial intensity. When co-present with other interfering ions, F− still exhibited almost the same quenching ability (39.3 %–43.0 %), demonstrating the high anti-interference performance and practical application potential of the approach (Fig. 4C).

To clarify the advantages of phosphorescence over fluorescence, the background phosphorescence and fluorescence signals in buffer, mouthwash, serum, and urine matrices were measured. It was observed that the background fluorescence signals in diluted mouthwash × 10, serum × 20, and urine × 20 were 1.7 (Colgate), 1.8 (LESENING), 4.8, and 28.7 times higher than that in HAc-NaAc buffer, respectively. Even 100-fold diluted urine still exhibited a background fluorescence signal approximately 15.5-fold higher than that of the buffer. The strong background fluorescence signals in urine and serum are likely attributed to the luminescent biomolecules such as proteins, while those in mouthwash may arise from added fluorescent molecules [10,13]. However, the background phosphorescence signals in these matrices remained as low as that of the HAc-NaAc buffer (Fig. 4D and S16), suggesting that the CDs@SiO2-based phosphorescence assay offers the advantage of avoiding interference from background signals. This characteristic potentially eliminates the need for extensive pretreatment in the analysis of complex samples, allowing for accurate determination of F− ions with high SNR and selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-based smart approach for F− detection
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to signals such as phosphorescence and fluorescence, colour signals (RGB values) are often used as response signals for target analysis, especially in remote and underdeveloped areas, making it easier and more convenient to achieve accurate detection of targets [39–42]. Therefore, a smart detection platform based on phosphorescence image G/R values was developed by combining this phosphorescence method with a portable camera obscura and a smartphone for the accurate detection of F− (Fig. 5A and S17). It should be noted that to ensure the stability of the image-based detection platform, the concentration of CDs@SiO2 was increased to 500 μg mL−1 to provide sufficient brightness. Furthermore, there is no significant difference in the G/R values of images captured with smartphone cameras of different brands, indicating its favorable robustness (Fig. S18). As demonstrated in Fig. 5 B, the phosphorescence intensity of the solutions decreased significantly with increasing F− concentrations. The G/R values of the phosphorescence image of the test solution show a favorable linear relationship with the F− concentration range of 0.1–12 mM with a LOD of 55.7 μM (S/N = 3) (Fig. 5C and D). It should be noted that while the maximum residue level (79 μM) specified by the WHO falls outside the linear range, concentrations ranging from 3 to 20 times the maximum residue level fall within this linear range. Therefore, spiked recovery experiments can be conducted to determine the F− content in the samples and evaluate whether the samples comply with the standard. In addition, the detection sensitivity can be further improved by combining the method with techniques such as magnetic separation [43]. Furthermore, the phosphorescent intensity of the CDs@SiO2 remained almost constant over ten irradiation cycles, indicating their favorable photostability (Fig. S19). The above results indicate that this potable platform holds great potential for the in situ F− detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of F− in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The constructed approach was applied to the determination of F− in real samples to examine its application potential. F− can strengthen the enamel against acid attack and help remineralize the enamel to repair the early stages of tooth decay, and is often added as a functional factor in mouthwash [44]. Therefore, the established approach was directly employed to determine the F− content in mouthwash. Without any pretreatments, 20 μL of Colgate and LESENING mouthwashes were added to the test system for F− content detection. As illustrated in Table 1 , the F− concentrations of the two types of mouthwashes were determined to be 1.07 and 1.43 mM, respectively, without significantly different from the reference values of 1.053 and 1.316 mM provided by the manufacturer (Fig. S20), confirming the accuracy and practical value of the method. Moreover, assessing F− in urine is an important means to evaluate the environmental F− exposure level and F− intake in the human body. Therefore, the suitability of the approach for the detection of F− in urine was investigated by the standard addition method. The recoveries of spiked standards in urine range from 96 % to 103 %, further indicating the satisfactory accuracy and robustness of the approach in complex real sample detection. Moreover, this method was successfully applied to the determination of F− in serum and tap water, confirming its reliability and wide applicability (Table 1 and Fig. S21).

In summary, we have developed a novel phosphorescence approach based on CDs@SiO2 for the detection of F− in complex aqueous samples. The phosphorescent approach integrates the advantages of none autofluorescence interference, high selectivity, and complete water compatibility, displaying high sensing accuracy and sensitivity with a detection liner range of 0.001–4 mM and a LOD of 1 μM for F−. Furthermore, a portable detection platform was constructed in combination with a portable camera obscura and a smartphone for the accurate detection of F− in mouthwash, urine, serum, and tap water, confirming its great potential in the portable detection of F−. Water-soluble phosphorescent probes are currently undergoing rapid development, and it is expected that the response rates and sensitivity can be further improved by designing phosphorescent probes with higher quantum yields for the in situ, accurate, and highly sensitive determination of F− in complex samples.

Meng-Xian Liu: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xiao-Bing Chen: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Wen-Ye Liu: Methodology, Data curation. Jian-Hua Wang: Supervision, Project administration. Yong-Liang Yu: Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Shuai Chen: Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21922402 and 21874017), Liaoning Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2021-MS-099), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N2205004). Special thanks are due to the instrumental analysis from Analytical and Testing Center, Northeastern University (China).
